title: File Handling
overview: Programs that only work with in-memory data are limited. File handling lets you save data permanently — read from files, write to files, and build applications that persist information.

blocks.title: Why Do We Need File Handling?
blocks.body: Without files, all data disappears when your program ends. Files let you save data permanently.
bullets: Variables disappear when the program closes — files persist
Files let you share data between different programs
Real applications save user data, settings, and logs to files
Python handles text files (.txt) and structured files (.csv, .json)

blocks.title: File Handling is Like a Notebook
blocks.text: Writing in a notebook saves information even after you close it. Open it tomorrow and the information is still there. File handling does the same for your programs.

blocks.title: Opening and Reading Files
blocks.body: Use the open() function to work with files. The default mode is 'read' (r).
bullets: file.read() — reads the entire file as one string
file.readlines() — reads all lines into a list

blocks.title: Reading a File
blocks.code:
# First create a file
with open("notes.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Line 1\nLine 2\nLine 3")

# Now read it
file = open("notes.txt", "r")
content = file.read()
print(content)
file.close()

blocks.title: The with Statement
blocks.body: The `with` statement automatically closes the file even if an error occurs.
bullets: with open("file.txt", "r") as f: — auto-closes when done
Safer and cleaner than manual open/close

blocks.title: Using with to Read
blocks.code:
with open("notes.txt", "r") as file:
    lines = file.readlines()
    for i, line in enumerate(lines, 1):
        print(f"{i}: {line}", end="")

blocks.title: Read and Greet
blocks.instructions: Write 3 names to students.txt. Read them back and greet each: 'Hello, [name]!'
blocks.starterCode:
with open("students.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Alice\nBob\nCharlie\n")

with open("students.txt", "r") as f:
    for name in f:
        print(f"Hello, {name.strip()}!")
blocks.solution:
with open("students.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Alice\nBob\nCharlie\n")

with open("students.txt", "r") as f:
    for name in f:
        print(f"Hello, {name.strip()}!")
blocks.hint: Use strip() on each line to remove the newline character.
blocks.expectedOutput:
Hello, Alice!
Hello, Bob!
Hello, Charlie!

blocks.title: Writing and Appending
blocks.body: Python has three main file modes: read (r), write (w), and append (a).
bullets: "a" mode — APPEND: adds to end of existing file
"r" mode — READ: reads from existing file
Use "w" carefully — it deletes existing content!

blocks.title: Write vs Append
blocks.code:
with open("diary.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Day 1: Started Python\n")

with open("diary.txt", "a") as f:
    f.write("Day 2: File handling!\n")

with open("diary.txt", "r") as f:
    print(f.read())
blocks.expectedOutput:
Day 1: Started Python
Day 2: File handling!

blocks.title: Notes Application
blocks.instructions: Ask for 3 notes, write them to a file, then read and display with line numbers.
blocks.starterCode:
filename = input("Filename: ")
n1 = input("Note 1: ")
n2 = input("Note 2: ")
n3 = input("Note 3: ")

with open(filename, "w") as f:
    f.write(n1 + "\n")
    f.write(n2 + "\n")
    f.write(n3 + "\n")

with open(filename, "r") as f:
    for i, line in enumerate(f, 1):
        print(f"{i}. {line}", end="")
blocks.solution:
filename = input("Filename: ")
n1 = input("Note 1: ")
n2 = input("Note 2: ")
n3 = input("Note 3: ")
with open(filename, "w") as f:
    f.write(n1 + "\n")
    f.write(n2 + "\n")
    f.write(n3 + "\n")
with open(filename, "r") as f:
    for i, line in enumerate(f, 1):
        print(f"{i}. {line}", end="")
blocks.hint: Use "w" mode. Add \\n at the end of each line when writing.
blocks.expectedOutput:
Filename: notes.txt
Note 1: Learn Python
Note 2: Practice daily
Note 3: Build projects
1. Learn Python
2. Practice daily
3. Build projects

keyTakeaways.text: Files persist data beyond program execution
Use open("file", "r") to read, "w" to write, "a" to append
Always close files — use with open() for automatic closing
"w" mode overwrites existing files — use carefully!
Append mode ("a") adds to the end without deleting

whatYouLearned: Why file handling is essential for data persistence
How to open files in read, write, and append modes
How to use with open() for automatic closing
How to read and write to files
How to build a simple notes application
